description.whyTrack: Network service scanning is a key reconnaissance indicator that precedes exploitation. UEBA can detect port scanning tools and built-in utilities used for service enumeration.
description.realWorldUsage: APT41, Iranian Chafer APT, Naikon APT, and Lebanese Cedar APT use netstat and port scanners to map network topology and identify vulnerable services.

description.whyTrack: Local account enumeration reveals potential targets for credential theft or privilege escalation. Unusual account queries from non-admin users are suspicious.
description.realWorldUsage: Used by virtually all threat actors during reconnaissance phase to identify privileged accounts and service accounts.

description.whyTrack: PowerShell is the most commonly abused execution method. Monitor for encoded commands, download cradles, and suspicious script execution patterns.
description.realWorldUsage: Used by APT29, APT41, FIN7, Lazarus, and virtually all modern threat actors. Cobalt Strike and other C2 frameworks heavily utilize PowerShell.

techniques.name: Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service
techniques.command: sc.exe create MagnetoSvc_$([guid]::NewGuid().ToString().Substring(0,8)) binPath= "cmd.exe /c echo Simulated" start= demand | Out-Null

description.whyTrack: Malicious service creation is a high-confidence persistence indicator. Monitor Event IDs 4697 and 7045 for suspicious service installations.
description.realWorldUsage: Used by Cobalt Strike, APT41, CARBANAK, GREYENERGY, and ransomware groups like DoppelPaymer and AvosLocker.

description.whyTrack: Defender exclusion modifications are high-priority alerts. Attackers add exclusions to allow malware execution in specific paths.
description.realWorldUsage: Common technique across ransomware operations including Conti, LockBit, and BlackCat. Also used by APT groups for stealth.

description.whyTrack: File deletion patterns can indicate evidence destruction. Monitor for bulk deletions, log clearing, and removal of security tool artifacts.
description.realWorldUsage: APT29 (GRAPELOADER), APT28, and most threat actors delete tools and logs post-compromise to hinder forensic analysis.

techniques.name: OS Credential Dumping: NTDS
techniques.command: ntdsutil.exe "ac i ntds" "ifm" "create full C:\Windows\Temp\MagnetoNTDS" q q 2>$null

description.whyTrack: NTDS.dit extraction enables offline cracking of all domain passwords. This is a critical alert requiring immediate response.
description.realWorldUsage: Lazarus (DEV#POPPER), APT29, APT28, and virtually all APT groups targeting domain environments extract NTDS.dit for credential access.

description.whyTrack: Network connection enumeration reveals active communications and potential lateral movement paths. Unusual queries from workstations are suspicious.
description.realWorldUsage: APT41 (Shadow Harvest) uses this for network reconnaissance to identify connected systems and active services.

description.whyTrack: Network configuration enumeration is standard reconnaissance. Multiple rapid queries or queries from unusual contexts are suspicious.
description.realWorldUsage: Universal technique used by all threat actors to understand network topology, DNS servers, and gateway information.

description.whyTrack: User context enumeration helps attackers understand privileges and plan escalation. Unusual whoami calls can indicate compromise.
description.realWorldUsage: Every threat actor uses this technique immediately after initial access to understand their current privilege level.

description.whyTrack: Scheduled task creation is a common persistence mechanism. Monitor for tasks with suspicious triggers, paths, or created by unusual users.
description.realWorldUsage: Lazarus (DEV#POPPER), APT29, and ransomware groups use scheduled tasks for persistence and delayed execution.

description.whyTrack: DLL side-loading abuses trusted applications to load malicious DLLs. Monitor for DLL loads from unusual paths alongside legitimate executables.
description.realWorldUsage: Lazarus (DEV#POPPER) and APT41 frequently use DLL side-loading with signed applications like legitimate software installers.

description.whyTrack: SMB share enumeration and access is key for lateral movement. Monitor for unusual share access patterns and admin share connections.
description.realWorldUsage: APT41, APT28, Lazarus, and virtually all threat actors use SMB for lateral movement via admin shares (C$, ADMIN$).

techniques.name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash

description.whyTrack: Pass-the-Hash bypasses password requirements using NTLM hashes. Monitor for NTLM authentication anomalies and logon type patterns.
description.realWorldUsage: APT41 (Shadow Harvest) and most APT groups use Pass-the-Hash with tools like Mimikatz for lateral movement.

description.whyTrack: Registry modifications can establish persistence, disable security, or store malware. Monitor sensitive registry locations.
description.realWorldUsage: StealthFalcon (Project Raven) and many threat actors modify registry for persistence and to disable security features.

techniques.name: Create Account: Local Account
techniques.command: net user MagnetoTestUser P@ssw0rd123! /add 2>$null; net user MagnetoTestUser /delete 2>$null

description.whyTrack: Local account creation for backdoor access is a critical indicator. Monitor Event ID 4720 for account creation outside normal processes.
description.realWorldUsage: Ransomware operators and APT groups create local admin accounts for persistent backdoor access.

description.whyTrack: Base64 encoding and other obfuscation techniques hide malicious payloads. Monitor for encoded PowerShell commands and suspicious encoding patterns.
description.realWorldUsage: StealthFalcon (Project Raven) and virtually all threat actors use encoding to evade signature-based detection.

techniques.name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: UAC Bypass

description.whyTrack: UAC bypass attempts indicate privilege escalation. Monitor for modifications to auto-elevate registry keys and fodhelper/eventvwr abuse.
description.realWorldUsage: StealthFalcon, APT28, and many threat actors use UAC bypass techniques for privilege escalation without triggering UAC prompts.

description.whyTrack: Token manipulation enables privilege escalation and impersonation. Monitor for unusual token operations and process creation with different tokens.
description.realWorldUsage: APT29 (GRAPELOADER) uses token manipulation for privilege escalation and to impersonate higher-privileged users.

techniques.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Port Monitors

description.whyTrack: Port monitor persistence is stealthy and rarely used legitimately. Any modifications to print monitor registry keys are highly suspicious.
description.realWorldUsage: Advanced threat actors use port monitors for persistence as they are rarely monitored and execute with SYSTEM privileges.

description.whyTrack: LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning captures credentials on the network. Monitor for tools like Responder and unusual broadcast traffic patterns.
description.realWorldUsage: Common penetration testing technique also used by threat actors for credential harvesting in enterprise environments.

techniques.name: Indirect Command Execution
techniques.command: forfiles /P C:\Windows /M notepad.exe /C "cmd /c echo test" 2>$null | Out-Null

description.whyTrack: Indirect execution via forfiles, pcalua, and other LOLBins bypasses application whitelisting. Monitor for unusual parent-child process relationships.
description.realWorldUsage: Used by sophisticated threat actors to evade application control solutions and endpoint detection.

description.whyTrack: Tool transfer indicates active C2 communication. Monitor for unusual download patterns, especially to temp directories or using encoded URLs.
description.realWorldUsage: FIN7 (Carbanak) and virtually all threat actors use various methods to transfer tools post-compromise.

description.whyTrack: Rundll32 abuse for script execution bypasses security controls. Monitor for unusual rundll32 command lines and child processes.
description.realWorldUsage: APT41 (Shadow Harvest) and many threat actors abuse rundll32 for fileless execution and defense evasion.

description.whyTrack: Data exfiltration over C2 is the final attack stage. Monitor for unusual data volumes over established C2 channels.
description.realWorldUsage: APT41 (Shadow Harvest) exfiltrates data over the same channels used for command and control to blend with normal traffic.

description.whyTrack: EPM poisoning is an advanced RPC hijacking technique. Monitor for unusual RPC endpoint registration and abnormal RPC traffic patterns.
description.realWorldUsage: APT41 Shadow Harvest campaign exploits EPM poisoning for NTLM relay attacks against SYSTEM-level services.

techniques.name: Event Triggered Execution: WMI Event Subscription

description.whyTrack: WMI event subscriptions provide fileless persistence. Monitor WMI activity and __EventFilter, __EventConsumer, __FilterToConsumerBinding classes.
description.realWorldUsage: APT29 (POSHSPY backdoor) and APT41 use WMI subscriptions for persistent, fileless backdoor access.

techniques.name: Archive Collected Data: Archive via Library

description.whyTrack: Data compression before exfiltration is a common pattern. Monitor for archive creation in staging directories and unusual compression activity.
description.realWorldUsage: Lazarus (DEV#POPPER) compresses collected data using RAR and ZIP before exfiltration to reduce transfer size.

description.whyTrack: C2 over legitimate web services evades network filtering. Monitor for unusual patterns in cloud service traffic (O365, SharePoint, etc.).
description.realWorldUsage: APT41 (Shadow Harvest) uses SharePoint and O365 for C2 communication to blend with legitimate enterprise traffic.

description.whyTrack: Regsvr32 abuse (Squiblydoo) enables script execution bypassing AppLocker. Monitor for regsvr32 with network or script URLs.
description.realWorldUsage: APT28 and other threat actors use regsvr32 with scriptlet files (.sct) for fileless execution.

description.whyTrack: BITS jobs can transfer files stealthily and persist across reboots. Monitor for unusual BITS jobs and transfers from suspicious URLs.
description.realWorldUsage: APT groups use BITS for stealthy file transfers that survive reboots and blend with Windows Update traffic.

description.whyTrack: Domain trust enumeration reveals paths for cross-domain attacks. Monitor for nltest and AD trust queries from unusual sources.
description.realWorldUsage: APT groups enumerate trusts to identify paths for lateral movement between trusted domains in enterprise environments.

description.whyTrack: Security software enumeration helps attackers plan evasion. Monitor for queries targeting AV, EDR, and security tool processes.
description.realWorldUsage: All sophisticated threat actors enumerate security tools before deploying payloads to select appropriate evasion techniques.

description.whyTrack: Firewall rule enumeration reveals network restrictions. Monitor for unusual firewall configuration queries and rule modifications.
description.realWorldUsage: Attackers enumerate firewall rules to understand network segmentation and plan lateral movement or data exfiltration.

techniques.name: Windows Management Instrumentation
techniques.command: Get-WmiObject Win32_OperatingSystem | Select-Object Caption, Version | Out-Null

description.whyTrack: WMI enables remote execution without deploying files. Monitor for remote WMI connections and process creation via WMI.
description.realWorldUsage: APT41 and many threat actors use WMI for lateral movement as it leaves minimal forensic artifacts.

techniques.name: System Services: Service Execution
techniques.command: sc.exe query state= all | Select-Object -First 20 | Out-Null

description.whyTrack: Remote service creation enables code execution on other systems. Monitor for remote sc.exe connections and service installations.
description.realWorldUsage: Cobalt Strike and other C2 frameworks use PSExec-style service execution for lateral movement.

techniques.name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel: HTTP POST

description.whyTrack: HTTP POST exfiltration blends with normal web traffic. Monitor for unusual POST data volumes and destinations.
description.realWorldUsage: Most threat actors exfiltrate data via HTTP/HTTPS POST requests to blend with normal enterprise web traffic.

description.whyTrack: DNS exfiltration bypasses many security controls. Monitor for high volumes of DNS queries, long subdomain names, and TXT record queries.
description.realWorldUsage: APT groups and financial threat actors use DNS tunneling for stealthy C2 and data exfiltration.

description.whyTrack: Ransomware encryption is a critical impact. Monitor for rapid file modifications, known ransomware extensions, and ransom notes.
description.realWorldUsage: All ransomware operations including LockBit, BlackCat, Royal, and Cl0p encrypt data for extortion.

description.whyTrack: Shadow copy deletion prevents recovery. Monitor for vssadmin, wmic, and bcdedit commands targeting backup infrastructure.
description.realWorldUsage: Ransomware operators delete shadow copies and backups before encryption to maximize impact and payment pressure.

description.whyTrack: Stopping security and backup services enables attacks. Monitor for service stop commands targeting security tools and databases.
description.realWorldUsage: Ransomware groups stop database, backup, and security services before encryption to ensure maximum file access.

description.whyTrack: Phishing attachments are the top initial access vector. Monitor for macro-enabled documents, LNK files, and unusual attachment types.
description.realWorldUsage: APT29, APT28, and virtually all threat actors use spearphishing with malicious attachments for initial access.

description.whyTrack: Compromised credentials enable stealthy access. Monitor for impossible travel, unusual login times, and abnormal access patterns.
description.realWorldUsage: All APT groups leverage stolen or compromised credentials to blend with legitimate user activity.

description.whyTrack: System enumeration reveals OS details, patches, and domain membership. Monitor for unusual systeminfo executions.
description.realWorldUsage: Universal reconnaissance technique used by all threat actors to understand the target environment.

description.whyTrack: File enumeration identifies valuable data. Monitor for recursive directory listings and searches for sensitive file types.
description.realWorldUsage: All threat actors enumerate files to identify sensitive data, credentials, and valuable intellectual property.

description.whyTrack: Remote system enumeration identifies lateral movement targets. Monitor for net view, ping sweeps, and AD queries.
description.realWorldUsage: APT groups enumerate network systems to identify high-value targets like domain controllers and file servers.

description.whyTrack: Local data collection precedes exfiltration. Monitor for unusual file access patterns and large-scale file reads.
description.realWorldUsage: All threat actors collect sensitive local data including documents, credentials, and configuration files.

techniques.name: Archive Collected Data: Archive via Utility

description.whyTrack: Data compression using utilities indicates preparation for exfiltration. Monitor for archive tool usage in unusual contexts.
description.realWorldUsage: Threat actors use native and third-party compression tools to stage data before exfiltration.

description.whyTrack: RDP is a common lateral movement method. Monitor for unusual RDP connections, especially from unexpected sources.
description.realWorldUsage: APT groups and ransomware operators use RDP for interactive access and lateral movement within networks.

description.whyTrack: System shutdown can disrupt operations and hide forensic evidence. Monitor for unexpected shutdown commands.
description.realWorldUsage: Ransomware may force reboots to complete encryption or clear memory-resident evidence.

description.whyTrack: Automated exfiltration uses scheduled tasks or scripts. Monitor for data transfer patterns and scheduled task execution.
description.realWorldUsage: APT groups automate data exfiltration to continuously steal data over extended periods.

description.whyTrack: Data staging in temp directories precedes exfiltration. Monitor for unusual directory creation and file movements.
description.realWorldUsage: Threat actors stage collected data in temporary directories before compression and exfiltration.

description.whyTrack: Exploitation of public services provides initial access. Monitor for unusual process spawning from web services.
description.realWorldUsage: APT groups exploit vulnerabilities in web applications, VPNs, and other internet-facing services.

description.whyTrack: External remote services like VPN and RDP provide persistent access. Monitor for unusual remote access patterns.
description.realWorldUsage: Threat actors maintain persistence through legitimate remote access services using stolen credentials.

In real-world attacks, adversaries use **T1591 (Gather Organization Information)** to study a companyâ€™s identity structure, domain relationships, and key personnel before launching targeted attacks. Campaigns like **SolarWinds, LAPSUS$, and FIN7** used organizational intelligence to identify high-value systems, trusted partners, and privileged users. This reconnaissance enabled more effective **spear-phishing, help-desk impersonation, and supply-chain compromise** rather than random exploitation. Without this phase, many modern intrusions would fail before reaching privileged access.
description.whyTrack: Tracking MITRE ATT&CK T1591 â€“ Gather Organization Information is not a â€œnice to haveâ€ â€” it is one of the strongest early-warning indicators of an attack before exploitation even begins. It gives SOC teams the earliest chance to break the kill chain while the attacker is still planning and mapping.
